Some of the strategies that I used was focusing on the different shapes on the input and the different shapes on the output. I asked myself, what shapes are being copied over from the input to the output. Another strategy was looking for the relationship between the input grid size and the output grid size and think about how the grid size changed sizes and what caused the grid size to change (i.e., what shapes inside the input caused the output to change to a certain size).

Another strategy was to look for sequences. There seemed to be some sequences that tended to pop up dependent upon the colors presented in the input. I also focused on the colors that were being transferred over from the input to the output, and what colors were being ignored.

Finally, I tried to focus only on the sections of the input that seemed to make a difference for the output and ignore any areas of the input that had no impact on the output. In many of the examples, it seemed that there was a section of the input that determined the output and a section of the input that was ignored in determining the output. I tried to zero in on these sections.

I thought that the experiment ran very smoothly and I did not appear to have any issues with the interface. Two of the problems were rather challenging, but I think I was on the right track. The first thing that I did for each problem was look at the similarities within all of the examples. If I could find something that occured the same or in the same manner within each example output, regardless of composition, I then had a general idea on how the rule worked. I then looked to see if there were any specific differences based upon positioning or amount of squares. This in turn gave me a better idea what particular elements of an output might alter the rulle a bit. After this, I compared what I thought was the rule, to the examples to see if my problem output followed the rule. Idealy, I would find an example that was closest to the problem in question. In this way, if I was mistaken about the rule, I would be able to focus more on how the current problem and its respective example worked. This would then help me to figure out what exactly I got wrong about the rule. In the first example, I attempted to do this, but there was clearly something that I was missing or not unerstanding about how the rule worked. Once I did all of these things, I would then make certain that the rule was constant across each example before making a submission.
